title: ADUs, Casitas, and Guest Houses in Arizona: The Complete Guide
summary: An accessory dwelling unit (ADU), casita, or guest house is a second living space on your lot, and which one you can build depends on Arizona's 2024 ADU laws and your city's rules. This guide covers what each term means, the statewide ADU mandate, per-city rules in Phoenix, Scottsdale, Mesa, and the northeast towns, cost, renting it out and the TPT tax, placement and design, the RV-garage casita, and the HOA and permit hurdles.
bodyHtml:
ADUs, Casitas, and Guest Houses in Arizona: The Complete Guide

An accessory dwelling unit (ADU), a casita, and a guest house are all second living spaces on a single lot, and the difference between them is the kitchen and whether you can rent it out. An ADU is a fully independent home with its own kitchen, bath, and entrance; a casita is the Southwest word for a small guest house, with or without a kitchen; a guest house without a kitchen is just bedroom space, not a separate dwelling. Since 2024, Arizona law under A.R.S. 9-461.18 requires larger cities to allow at least one ADU on most single-family lots, which has reshaped what you can build. This guide covers what you can build, where, what it costs, and the rules on renting, placement, and design, with a deep page behind every branch.

What this guide covers

  • ADU vs. casita vs. guest house: the definitions
  • Arizona's statewide ADU law
  • Per-city ADU and casita rules
  • Setbacks, size, and how many you can build
  • What an ADU or casita costs
  • Renting it out and the TPT tax
  • Placement and design in your plan
  • The RV-garage casita
  • HOA limits and the permit hurdles
  • Property taxes and cooling

ADU vs. casita vs. guest house: the definitions

Get the terms straight first, because the kitchen is the legal line that decides everything else. An ADU is a complete second dwelling: kitchen, bathroom, sleeping area, and its own entrance, which makes it legally rentable as a separate home. A casita is the Arizona term for a small guest house and may or may not have a kitchen. A guest house without a kitchen is not an independent dwelling, which keeps it out of some rules but also means you cannot legally rent it as a separate unit.

Start with what is an ADU in Arizona? and the side-by-side casita vs. guest house vs. ADU in Arizona . The kitchen question is settled in can I build a guest house without a kitchen in Arizona? . The glossary defines a casita and the junior ADU (JADU) , a smaller ADU type carved out of the main house.

Arizona's statewide ADU law

Arizona now requires most larger cities to allow at least one ADU on single-family lots, under a 2024 law codified at A.R.S. 9-461.18 for municipalities and A.R.S. 11-810.01 for counties. The municipal statute applies to cities above a population threshold and limits how restrictive their ADU rules can be, including caps on owner-occupancy requirements and parking demands. This is the single biggest change to Arizona ADU law in a generation, and it is why a casita that was banned in 2023 may be permitted today.

Learn the requirements in Arizona ADU requirements and check whether your city is covered in which Arizona cities are required to allow ADUs? . For the statutes themselves, the reference library carries A.R.S. 9-461.18 (municipal ADU law) and A.R.S. 11-810.01 (county ADU law) .

Per-city ADU and casita rules

Confirm your specific city's rules, because the statewide law sets a floor while each city still controls setbacks, height, design, and short-term-rental limits. Phoenix, Scottsdale, and Mesa each adopted their own ADU ordinances to comply with state law, and the smaller northeast towns of Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree handle casitas differently again on their larger lots. The rules that vary most are maximum size, required setbacks, and whether the unit can be rented short-term.

See the big-city rules in ADU and casita rules in Phoenix , ADU and casita rules in Scottsdale , and ADU and casita rules in Mesa . For the northeast towns, read do Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs? .

Setbacks, size, and how many you can build

Check the setback, size cap, and unit count before you design, because these three numbers decide whether your casita fits the lot at all. Most Arizona cities cap an ADU at a share of the main home's floor area or a fixed square footage, hold it to side and rear setbacks, and limit how many accessory units one lot can have. The statewide law pushed some of these limits in the homeowner's favor, but the exact figures still come from your city's zoning code.

Work the dimensions with ADU setback and size rules by city in Arizona and the unit count with how many ADUs per property in Arizona? . The glossary explains the setback and lot coverage limits that constrain placement.

What an ADU or casita costs

Budget an ADU or casita as a small house, not an add-on, because it needs its own foundation, roof, and connections to power, water, and sewer or septic. Cost per square foot usually runs higher than the main home because the fixed costs of a kitchen, bathroom, and utility hookups spread over far fewer square feet. Site work, whether the unit is attached or detached, and the level of finish move the number most.

Price a detached casita with cost to build a casita in Arizona and an independent ADU with cost to build an ADU in Arizona . If you are adding one to an existing home, see how to add a casita or detached structure to an existing home in Arizona .

Renting it out and the TPT tax

You can rent a true ADU as a long-term or short-term rental in most Arizona cities, but a short-term rental triggers Arizona's transaction privilege tax (TPT) and a license. A guest house without a kitchen generally cannot be rented as a separate dwelling, which is the practical reason to build a full ADU if rental income is the goal. Cities can regulate short-term rentals through licensing and zoning even where the state limits outright bans, so confirm both the city rule and the tax registration.

Start with can I rent out my casita or ADU in Scottsdale? and the conversion path in can I convert a guest house into a rentable ADU in Arizona? . Handle the tax with do I need a TPT license for a short-term rental in Arizona? .

Placement and design in your plan

Place the casita where it gives the guest privacy without cutting off the main home's yard, light, or views, and design it to cool independently. In a new custom home, the casita can sit across a courtyard, off the motor court, or above a garage, and each placement changes privacy, plumbing runs, and how the two buildings share outdoor space. A bedroom in any casita must meet the building code's egress rule, a window or door large enough to escape and be rescued through, which is a common plan-review trip-up.

Place it well with where should you place a casita or guest suite in a custom home layout? . Avoid the egress rejection explained in why did the plan reviewer reject my casita or guest house bedroom egress? . For where a detached casita fits the broader plan, see our custom home design guide .

The RV-garage casita

You can build living quarters above or beside an RV garage in Arizona, which combines vehicle storage and a guest casita in one structure. This is popular on the larger lots of Rio Verde, Cave Creek, and Apache Junction, where an owner wants both a tall RV bay and a guest suite. The casita portion still has to meet ADU or guest-house rules for size, setbacks, and egress, even though it shares a roof with the garage.

See how it works in can you build a casita above or beside an RV garage in Arizona? . The glossary defines the RV garage and the casita that pairs with it.

HOA limits and the permit hurdles

Check your HOA before you count on a city approval, because an HOA can restrict accessory dwellings even where the city and state allow them. Many Arizona planned communities limit detached structures, rentals, or design through their CC&Rs and design review, and those private rules sit on top of the public zoning code. Clearing the city permit is only half the job if a homeowners association governs your lot.

Settle the conflict with can my HOA block an ADU if the city allows it in Arizona? . The glossary explains the CC&Rs and the design review committee that enforce them. The HOA review process for any custom build is covered in how does HOA design review affect building a custom home? .

Property taxes and cooling

Adding a casita raises your assessed value, and therefore your property tax, because it adds permitted, livable square footage that the county assessor picks up. The increase is usually modest relative to the cost of the unit, but it is real and ongoing. The other running cost is cooling: a casita used part-time should be on its own zone or system so you are not air-conditioning an empty guest house all summer.

See the tax effect in does adding a casita or addition raise my property taxes in Arizona? and the cooling solution in how do you cool a casita or guest suite separately from the main house in Arizona? .

The takeaway

An ADU, casita, or guest house is a small second home, and the kitchen decides what it legally is and whether you can rent it. Arizona's 2024 ADU law forced larger cities to allow at least one ADU on most lots, but your city, your HOA, and the building code's egress and setback rules still set the real limits. Each section above links to the deep page with the specific numbers and rules, and the broader custom home design guide shows how a casita fits a whole plan. Build it as a real house, confirm the city and HOA rules first, and a casita can add living space, rental income, or room for family that grows with you.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 9-461.18 (Accessory dwelling units; municipalities; the statewide ADU mandate)
  • A.R.S. 11-810.01 (Accessory dwelling units; counties)
  • City of Scottsdale: Accessory Dwelling Units (development standards)

title: Building a Custom Home in Apache Junction: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Apache Junction means designing to the city's 2018 I-codes and 2017 NEC, paying a flat $6,736 development impact fee plus a valuation-based permit fee, and working a desert lot on the Pinal and Maricopa County edge. Expect about $225 to $400 per square foot in 2026, with land cheaper than north Scottsdale. The flat impact fee and the lower lot cost shape the budget.
bodyHtml:
Building a custom home in Apache Junction means designing to the city's 2018 family of International Codes and the 2017 National Electrical Code, paying a flat $6,736 development impact fee plus a valuation-based permit fee, and working a desert lot that sits on the Pinal and Maricopa County edge. A build typically runs $225 to $400 per square foot in 2026, with land cheaper than north Scottsdale. The flat impact fee and the lower lot cost are the two facts that shape the budget most.

This guide is the Apache Junction hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Apache Junction straddles the Pinal and Maricopa county line, so confirming which county your desert parcel falls in matters for the septic and well approvals that run through the county, not the city.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Apache Junction
  • Adopted building codes
  • The flat impact fee and permit fees
  • Single-residence zoning and setbacks
  • Desert lots and the Pinal-Maricopa edge
  • Well and septic on a desert lot
  • Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Builder, financing, and the construction loan
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Apache Junction questions

Cost to build in Apache Junction

A custom home in Apache Junction runs about $225 to $400 per square foot to build in 2026, with land cheaper than north Scottsdale. The city adds a flat $6,736 development impact fee for a single-family home, plus a valuation-based building permit fee, both due before the permit issues. The lower lot cost does most of the work in the gap with the north metro, since labor and materials are much the same across the region. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Apache Junction and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2018 I-codes)

Effective July 18, 2019, Apache Junction builds to the 2018 IRC and the rest of the 2018 I-code family, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, adopted by the City Council on June 18, 2019, and written into Chapter 7 of the City Code. That keeps the city on the same cycle as much of Pinal County and a step behind Phoenix's 2024 codes. A 2018 detail and a 2024 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs, so a set drawn for a Phoenix lot is not automatically right here.

Because Apache Junction sits on the county edge, the first step on any parcel is confirming whether it is inside the city or in unincorporated Pinal or Maricopa County, since that decides the code and the permit office. For the full code list and the local edits, see Apache Junction adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

The flat impact fee and permit fees

Apache Junction charges a new single-family home a flat development impact fee of $6,736, made of a $3,250 streets fee, a $1,707 parks fee, a $1,229 police fee, and a $550 library fee, collected at the first building permit. The building permit fee itself runs off the ICC Building Valuation Data table, separate from the impact fee. Arizona caps impact fees to the cost of growth-related infrastructure under A.R.S. 9-463.05. The flat structure makes Apache Junction easy to budget, but confirm the live numbers before you rely on them.

The single flat impact fee is the local quirk worth knowing, because it does not scale with house size the way a valuation-based fee does, so a larger home spreads that fixed cost over more square feet. See Apache Junction building permit and impact fees , the impact fees glossary entry , and building permit cost in Arizona .

Single-residence zoning and setbacks

Apache Junction single-residence zoning runs from RS-GR on 1.25-acre lots down to RS-5 on 5,000 square foot lots, and Table 5-2 sets each yard. RS-GR needs a 40-foot front, 20-foot sides, and a 40-foot rear yard. RS-7 needs a 20-foot front, 7-foot sides, and a 20-foot rear. Knowing your district before you design tells you the real buildable box, not just the lot lines.

The deep RS-GR setbacks suit the large desert lots common on the edge of town, while the tighter RS-7 and RS-5 yards fit the platted subdivisions. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Apache Junction residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Desert lots and the Pinal-Maricopa edge

Apache Junction sits where Pinal and Maricopa counties meet, so a desert lot near the edge of town can fall in either county once you leave the city limits. The desert terrain means grading, drainage, and access can add cost before the house starts, and a sloped parcel toward the Superstition Mountains needs more site work than a flat one. Which county the parcel sits in decides where septic and well approvals are filed. The first question on any edge parcel is the jurisdiction. See how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county .

Well and septic on a desert lot

A new home on a desert lot outside city utilities needs two separate approvals: a septic permit and a private well authorized by the state through an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill. On a Pinal County parcel, the septic permit comes from the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, which ADEQ delegates to the county, and on lots of 5 acres or fewer the county also reviews the well. A Maricopa County parcel files septic through Maricopa County Environmental Services instead. The septic side starts with a soils evaluation and perc test.

Inside the city, many lots have municipal water and sewer at the street, which removes the well and septic step. On a desert parcel, water and wastewater are the two approvals most likely to slip the schedule. See Pinal County septic and well requirements , Maricopa County septic and well requirements , whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona , and how close a well can be to a septic system .

Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon

An Apache Junction custom home is designed first for heat, expansive soil, and monsoon storms, the same desert forces that shape every build at the east edge of the metro. Long stretches of 110-plus degree summer days push toward deep roof overhangs, low-gain glass, heavy insulation, and a high-SEER heat pump sized for the load. Many desert lots sit on soils that swell and shrink with moisture, so a soils report and an engineered foundation, often a post-tensioned slab, keep the slab from cracking as the ground moves. Monsoon rain arrives fast in July and August and can sheet off the nearby Superstition foothills, so site drainage has to move water away from the slab.

These forces shape the structure more than the floor plan does, and a soils report tells you which foundation the lot needs before you price the build. See whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona and the expansive soil glossary entry .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Apache Junction inspects a new home in stages from foundation through final, and each stage must pass before the next is covered up. After the final inspection passes, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy. On a desert well-and-septic lot, the inspection list also includes the septic system and the water setup, which the county signs off on separately from the city's building inspections. The permit submittals and the field process are in Apache Junction residential building permit requirements and the Apache Junction inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

A desert lot on the edge of town may not have utilities at the property line, so extending power and building a driveway and access can add real cost and lead time before the house starts. Power runs, a meter spot, and a graded access road are early-schedule items on a desert parcel, alongside the well and septic. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The first move on an Apache Junction custom home is confirming the lot's county, zoning, water source, and utility access before you buy, because those set both your buildable area and your budget. Confirm whether the parcel is inside the city or in unincorporated Pinal or Maricopa County, since that decides the code and where septic is filed. Before a footing pours, the lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and you call 811 before digging. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Builder, financing, and the construction loan

You pick a licensed builder and line up financing before the design locks, because a lender's appraisal and draw schedule shape the budget and the timeline. Verify any builder's license with the Arizona Registrar of Contractors before you sign, since the ROC license is what backs the implied warranty on your home. Most custom homes use a construction-to-permanent loan that funds the build in draws and then converts to a regular mortgage at completion, which keeps you to one closing. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC and the construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Apache Junction questions

The most common Apache Junction questions are about the per-foot cost, the flat impact fee, and the project timeline. Start with how much it costs to build a custom home in Apache Junction and building permit cost in Arizona . For the whole project arc, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the City of Apache Junction Development Services Fees . The cap on development fees is set in Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 , septic on rural land runs through Pinal County Wells and Septic , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and zoning rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the City of Apache Junction and the right county before you rely on them.

Sources

  • City of Apache Junction Development Services Fees
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)
  • Pinal County Wells and Septic (Aquifer Protection)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Building a Custom Home in Carefree: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Carefree means designing to the town's 2018 I-codes and 2017 NEC, putting in a private well or hauled water and a septic system on most lots, and fitting a home inside deep rural yards under a strict 24-foot height cap. Expect about $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, with desert estates passing $850. The lot and any design-review community drive the cost.
bodyHtml:
Building a custom home in Carefree means designing to the town's 2018 family of International Codes and the 2017 National Electrical Code, putting in a private well or hauled water and a septic system on most lots, and fitting a home inside deep rural yards under a strict 24-foot height cap. A build typically runs $350 to $700 per square foot in 2026, with high-end desert estates passing $850. The big local cost drivers are the lot itself: a well or hauled water, a septic system, desert and hillside site work, and the town's large required yards. In a community like Carefree Ranch, a design-review committee adds its own height, mass, and fee rules on top of the town's.

This guide is the Carefree hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Carefree runs older codes than newer neighbors like Cave Creek and Fountain Hills, so the exact rules differ depending on which town line your lot sits inside.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Carefree
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Rural zoning, setbacks, and the 24-foot height cap
  • Carefree Ranch and Lone Mountain design review
  • Well and septic on a Carefree lot
  • Desert and hillside site work
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Carefree questions

Cost to build in Carefree

A custom home in Carefree typically runs $350 to $700 per square foot to build in 2026, with high-end desert estates passing $850. The lot is the main story. A private well or hauled water, a septic system, desert and hillside site work, and the town's deep-yard zoning all add to the all-in cost beyond the per-foot finish number. In a design-review community like Carefree Ranch, plan and building-envelope fees and a strict height cap shape the budget too. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Carefree and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2018 I-codes)

Effective January 24, 2023, Carefree builds to the 2018 IRC and the rest of the 2018 I-code family, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, adopted by Ordinance 2022-02 and written into Town Code Section 10-1-2. That puts Carefree a cycle or two behind newer neighbors: Cave Creek runs the 2024 codes and 2023 NEC, so a plan drawn for a Cave Creek lot is not automatically right for a Carefree lot, and the reverse is also true. One wrinkle to confirm: the town's older handout once listed a 1994 plumbing code, but the codified Town Code adopts the 2018 IPC, which controls.

Because the town's editions differ from the surrounding county and from newer towns next door, the first question on any Carefree-area parcel is which jurisdiction it actually falls in, since that decides the code, the permit office, and the inspections. For the full code list and the per-code amendments, see Carefree adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city . For the plain-language version, see what building codes apply in Carefree .

Permit and impact fees

Carefree prices a new-home permit by construction valuation, with the permit-fee math and amendments set in Town Code Section 10-1-2. The town's posted valuation table can be dated, so confirm the live fee before you budget. See Carefree building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing compares to per-foot cities, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot . For the metro-wide picture, see building permit cost in Arizona .

Rural zoning, setbacks, and the 24-foot height cap

Carefree's residential zones require deep yards and cap building height at 24 feet across every district. Rural-70 needs a 60-foot front and rear yard and 30-foot sides at 13 percent lot coverage. Rural-43 drops to a 40-foot front and rear at 17 percent, and the smaller R1-18 needs a 30-foot front and 10-foot sides at 25 percent coverage. The town's main residential districts run from Rural-190 (over four acres) down to R1-10 (10,000 square feet).

The 24-foot height cap is the number that surprises buyers most, because it is lower than the 30-foot ceiling common in nearby cities and it shapes the whole design. Combined with deep front and rear yards on a large lot, it points most Carefree homes toward a spread-out single level or a carefully massed two-story that stays under the cap. Knowing your district before you design tells you the real buildable box, not just the lot lines. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Carefree residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Carefree Ranch and Lone Mountain design review

If your lot is in Carefree Ranch in the Village of Lone Mountain, a design review committee (DRC) adds rules on top of the town's. Carefree Ranch caps a home at 24 feet above natural grade at any point, breaks the house into building masses of 16 to 17.5 feet, and sets 60-foot street and 35-foot side setbacks. You must finish the exterior within 549 days and pay about $1,500 in plan and building-envelope fees. These private rules run alongside the town's permit, not instead of it, so you clear both.

The sloping-height rule is the trap here. Measuring 24 feet at any point above natural grade, not just at the lowest corner, means a downhill stretch of roof has to step with the terrain rather than run flat. That favors a massed, terraced design over a single tall box. For the full DRC rule set, see Carefree Ranch (Village of Lone Mountain) design guidelines . For how private review works in general, see how HOA design review affects building a custom home , how much HOA design-review fees run , how tall a home can be in a gated or hillside community , and whether you must use an approved builder list .

Well and septic on a Carefree lot

Most Carefree custom lots have no town sewer, so a home needs a septic system permitted by Maricopa County Environmental Services and either a private well authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources or a hauled-water setup with onsite storage. The septic side starts with a soils evaluation and perc test; the well side starts with a Notice of Intent to Drill filed with ADWR. These approvals run on their own timelines, so they belong early in the schedule.

Water and wastewater are the two approvals most likely to slip a Carefree schedule, because neither runs through the town. A perc test can fail or call for a larger, costlier septic field on tight desert soils, and a well can come in low or dry. Many builders confirm water and soils before they finalize the house design, since the results can move the building pad. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements , whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona , and how close a well can be to a septic system .

Desert and hillside site work

Carefree lots are desert and often sloped, so grading, drainage, retaining, and access can add real cost before the house starts. A hillside parcel needs engineered foundations and cut-and-fill work that a flat lot does not, and desert drainage rules shape the site plan. The 24-foot sloping-height cap in Carefree Ranch interacts with the terrain to set where and how tall the house can sit. The cost effect is detailed in how much it costs to build in Carefree , and the buildable-area limits flow from Carefree residential zoning and setbacks .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Carefree inspects a new home in stages from foundation through final, and each stage must pass before the next is covered up. You request inspections through the town's Permit and License Portal, and a request must be in by 4 p.m. for the next available day. After the final inspection passes, the town issues a Certificate of Occupancy. On a well-and-septic lot, the inspection list also includes the septic system and the water setup, which the county signs off on separately from the town's building inspections. The permit submittals and the field process are in Carefree residential building permit requirements and the Carefree inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

A Carefree desert lot rarely has utilities at the property line, so extending power and building a driveway and access can add real cost and lead time before the house starts. Power runs, a meter spot, and a graded access road are early-schedule items on a rural parcel, alongside the well and septic. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The first move on a Carefree custom home is confirming the lot's zoning, water source, slope, and any HOA design rules before you buy, because rural setbacks, the 24-foot height cap, the well-or-haul water question, and the terrain set both your buildable area and your budget. Confirm whether the parcel sits in the town or in the unincorporated county next door, since that decides the code. Before a footing pours, the lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and you call 811 before digging. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Carefree questions

The most common Carefree questions are about codes and accessory dwellings. Start with what building codes apply in Carefree for the plain-language code answer, and whether Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs for accessory dwellings. For the whole project arc, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the Town of Carefree Building Department and the codified Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 . Septic requirements are published by Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, zoning rules, and HOA design guidelines change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the Town of Carefree and your community's design committee before you rely on them.

Sources

  • Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 (Building Code Amendments)
  • Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater (Septic) Forms and Applications

title: Building a Custom Home in Casa Grande: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Casa Grande means designing to the city's 2018 I-codes and 2017 NEC, paying a permit priced off the ICC valuation table plus a 65 percent plan review fee, and budgeting separate impact fees due before the permit issues. Expect about $225 to $400 per square foot in 2026, lower than north Scottsdale because Pinal County land costs less. The cheaper lot is the main reason the all-in number drops.
bodyHtml:
Building a custom home in Casa Grande means designing to the city's 2018 family of International Codes and the 2017 National Electrical Code, paying a permit priced off the ICC Building Valuation Table plus a 65 percent plan review fee, and budgeting separate development impact fees due before the permit issues. A build typically runs $225 to $400 per square foot in 2026, lower than north Scottsdale because Pinal County land and lots cost less. The cheaper lot is the main reason the all-in number drops compared with the north metro.

This guide is the Casa Grande hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Casa Grande is an incorporated Pinal County city, so the city runs the building permits and inspections, while septic and well approvals on rural land run through Pinal County.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Casa Grande
  • Adopted building codes
  • Permit fees and the ICC valuation table
  • Development impact fees
  • Single-family zoning and setbacks
  • Well and septic on a rural Pinal County lot
  • Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Power, access, and rural utilities
  • Builder, financing, and the construction loan
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Common Casa Grande questions

Cost to build in Casa Grande

A custom home in Casa Grande runs about $225 to $400 per square foot to build in 2026, lower than north Scottsdale because Pinal County land and lots cost less. The labor and materials are much the same across the metro, so the lot price does most of the work in the gap. Plan on a city permit, the 65 percent plan review fee, and separate development impact fees on top of the per-foot build. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Casa Grande and, for the metro baseline, cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona .

Adopted building codes (2018 I-codes)

Casa Grande builds to the 2018 IRC and the rest of the 2018 I-code family, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, adopted by reference in Title 15 of the City Code with local amendments made public record by Resolution No. 5170. That keeps Casa Grande on the same cycle as many Pinal and outer-metro jurisdictions, a step behind Phoenix's 2024 codes. A 2018 detail and a 2024 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs, so a set drawn for a Phoenix lot is not automatically right here.

The first step on any parcel near the city edge is confirming whether it sits inside Casa Grande or in unincorporated Pinal County, since that decides the code and the permit office. For the full code list and the local edits, see Casa Grande adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit fees and the ICC valuation table

Casa Grande prices a new-home permit by construction valuation, using the latest ICC Building Valuation Table on a tiered fee scale. Building plan review is 65 percent of the permit fee, plus a 5 percent technology recovery fee. Because the valuation table is updated periodically, the posted numbers can lag, so confirm the live fee before you budget. See Casa Grande building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing compares to per-foot cities, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot . For the metro-wide picture, see building permit cost in Arizona .

Development impact fees

Separate from the permit, Casa Grande charges development impact fees set in the city's Infrastructure Improvements Plan, and they are due before the permit issues. Arizona caps these fees to the cost of growth-related infrastructure under A.R.S. 9-463.05, so they fund roads, parks, and public safety capacity tied to new homes rather than general city spending. The statute also requires the city to study and publish its fee math, which is why the numbers live in the Infrastructure Improvements Plan and get refreshed on a set cycle. The exact total changes as the city updates that plan, so a number you saw a year ago may not be current. Confirm the live amount with the city before you finalize the budget, because the impact fee and the valuation-based permit fee together can add several thousand dollars on top of the per-foot build. See Casa Grande building permit and impact fees and the impact fees glossary entry .

Single-family zoning and setbacks

Casa Grande's R-1 single-family zone needs a 7,000 square foot lot with a 15-foot front yard, a 20-foot setback for a street-facing garage, a 5 to 10-foot side yard, and a 20-foot rear yard. Larger rural zones run deeper, and the maximum building height across residential zones is 28 feet. Knowing your district before you design tells you the real buildable box, not just the lot lines.

The 20-foot garage setback is the detail that catches buyers off guard, since it pushes the house back farther than the 15-foot front yard alone would suggest. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Casa Grande residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona , and for the general ceiling, how tall a custom home can be in Arizona .

Well and septic on a rural Pinal County lot

A new home on rural land outside city utilities needs two separate approvals: a septic permit from the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, which ADEQ delegates to the county, and a private well authorized by the state through an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill. On lots of 5 acres or fewer, the county reviews the well. The septic side starts with a soils evaluation and perc test; the well side starts with the Notice of Intent. These approvals run on their own timelines, so they belong early in the schedule.

Inside the city, many lots have municipal water and sewer at the street, which removes the well and septic step. On a rural parcel, water and wastewater are the two approvals most likely to slip the schedule, since neither runs through the city. See Pinal County septic and well requirements , whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona , and how close a well can be to a septic system .

Designing for heat, soil, and monsoon

A Casa Grande custom home is designed first for heat, expansive soil, and monsoon storms, the same desert forces that shape every Pinal and Maricopa County build. Long stretches of 110-plus degree summer days push toward deep roof overhangs, low-gain glass, heavy insulation, and a high-SEER heat pump sized for the load. Many lots in the area sit on soils that swell and shrink with moisture, so a soils report and an engineered foundation, often a post-tensioned slab, keep the slab from cracking as the ground moves. Monsoon rain arrives fast in July and August, so site drainage has to move water away from the slab.

These forces shape the structure more than the floor plan does, and a soils report tells you which foundation the lot needs before you price the build. See whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona and the expansive soil glossary entry .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Casa Grande inspects a new home in stages from foundation through final, and each stage must pass before the next is covered up. After the final inspection passes, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy. On a rural well-and-septic lot, the inspection list also includes the septic system and the water setup, which the county signs off on separately from the city's building inspections. The permit submittals and the field process are in Casa Grande residential building permit requirements and the Casa Grande inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

A rural lot outside the city core may not have utilities at the property line, so extending power and building a driveway and access can add real cost and lead time before the house starts. Power runs, a meter spot, and a graded access road are early-schedule items on a rural parcel, alongside the well and septic. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The first move on a Casa Grande custom home is confirming the lot's zoning, water source, and utility access before you buy, because those set both your buildable area and your budget. Confirm whether the parcel is inside the city or in unincorporated Pinal County, since that decides the code and the permit office. Before a footing pours, the lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and you call 811 before digging. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Builder, financing, and the construction loan

You pick a licensed builder and line up financing before the design locks, because a lender's appraisal and draw schedule shape the budget and the timeline. Verify any builder's license with the Arizona Registrar of Contractors before you sign, since the ROC license is what backs the implied warranty on your home. Most custom homes use a construction-to-permanent loan that funds the build in draws and then converts to a regular mortgage at completion, which keeps you to one closing. See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC and the construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Casa Grande questions

The most common Casa Grande questions are about the per-foot cost, the permit math, and the project timeline. Start with how much it costs to build a custom home in Casa Grande and building permit cost in Arizona . For the whole project arc, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the City of Casa Grande: the City Fee Schedule and Building Inspections . The cap on development fees is set in Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 , and Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, and zoning rules change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the City of Casa Grande and Pinal County before you rely on them.

title: Building a Custom Home in Cave Creek: The Complete Local Guide

Building a custom home in Cave Creek means designing to the town's new 2024 family of International Codes, putting in a private well or hauled water and a septic system on most lots, and fitting a home inside the town's deep-yard Desert Rural zoning. A build typically runs $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with high-end desert estates passing $800. The big local cost drivers are the lot itself: a well or hauled water, a septic system, desert and hillside site work, and the town's large required yards.

This guide is the Cave Creek hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Cave Creek runs newer codes than the unincorporated county next door, so the exact rules differ depending on which side of the town line your lot sits.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Cave Creek

Cost to build in Cave Creek

A custom home in Cave Creek typically runs $325 to $650 per square foot to build in 2026, with high-end desert estates passing $800. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) The lot is the main story. A private well or hauled water, a septic system, desert and hillside site work, and the town's deep-yard zoning all add to the all-in cost beyond the per-foot finish number. For the full breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Cave Creek .

Adopted building codes (2024 I-codes)

Effective January 1, 2026, Cave Creek builds to the 2024 IRC and most of the 2024 I-code family, plus the 2023 National Electrical Code and a few 2025-edition codes, adopted by Resolution R2025-01. One wrinkle: for homes, Cave Creek keeps the 2018 residential energy code rather than the 2024 energy code, which keeps the energy paperwork lighter. Because the town sits ahead of the surrounding unincorporated county (still on the 2018 codes and 2017 NEC), a set drawn for a rural county lot is not automatically right for a town lot.

This town-versus-county gap is the trap to watch on a Cave Creek build. Lots near the edge of town can sit just inside or just outside the boundary, and the code, the permit office, and the inspections all change with it. A plan drawn to the county's 2018 IRC and 2017 NEC will not match the town's 2024 codes and 2023 NEC, and the reverse is also true. So the first question on any Cave Creek parcel is which jurisdiction it falls in, since that decides everything downstream. For the full code list and the per-code amendments, see Cave Creek adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit and impact fees

Cave Creek prices a new-home permit by construction valuation, using the ICC Building Valuation Table set in Town Code Section 151.14, and plan review is a set minimum or a percentage of the permit fee, whichever is greater. The town's posted valuation table can be dated, so confirm the live fee before you budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Cave Creek building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing compares to per-foot cities, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot .

Desert Rural zoning and setbacks

Cave Creek's Desert Rural (DR) zones require big yards. DR-190, DR-89, and DR-70 each need a 60 foot front and rear yard and a 30 foot side yard, capped at 10 percent lot coverage. DR-43 drops to 40 foot front and rear and 20 foot sides at 15 percent, and building height tops out at 25 feet. These deep setbacks and low coverage caps are why Cave Creek homes sit far back on large desert parcels.

The 10 percent lot-coverage cap in the larger DR districts is the number that surprises buyers most. On a one-acre lot it points toward a building footprint of only a few thousand square feet under roof, which pushes larger homes toward a second story or a spread-out single level inside the deep setbacks. The 60 foot front and rear yards also mean a long driveway and more site work than a typical city lot. Knowing your district before you design tells you the real buildable box, not just the lot lines. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Cave Creek residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

Well and septic on a Cave Creek lot

Most Cave Creek custom lots have no town sewer or water, so a home needs a septic system permitted by Maricopa County Environmental Services and either a private well authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources or a hauled-water setup with onsite storage. The septic side starts with a soils evaluation and perc test; the well side starts with a Notice of Intent to Drill filed with ADWR. These approvals run on their own timelines, so they belong early in the schedule.

Water and wastewater are the two approvals most likely to slip a Cave Creek schedule, because neither runs through the town. A perc test can fail or call for a larger, costlier septic field on tight desert soils, and a well can come in low or dry, which changes your whole water plan. Drilling cost varies with depth, so two neighboring lots can have very different well budgets. Many builders confirm water and soils before they finalize the house design, since the results can move the building pad. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements and whether you need a soil test to build a house in Arizona .

Desert and hillside site work

Cave Creek lots are desert and often sloped, so grading, drainage, retaining, and access can add real cost before the house starts. A hillside parcel needs engineered foundations and cut-and-fill work that a flat lot does not, and desert drainage rules shape the site plan. The town's deep-yard zoning interacts with the terrain to set where the house can sit. The cost effect of desert and hillside site work is detailed in how much it costs to build in Cave Creek , and the buildable-area limits flow from Cave Creek residential zoning and setbacks .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Cave Creek inspects a new home in stages from foundation through final, and each stage must pass before the next is covered up. After the final inspection passes, the town issues a Certificate of Occupancy. On a well-and-septic lot, the inspection list also includes the septic system and the water setup, which the county signs off on separately from the town's building inspections. Plan the stages so the right approval is in hand before each cover-up, since a missed sign-off can mean opening work back up. The permit submittals and the field process are in Cave Creek residential building permit requirements and the Cave Creek inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Power, access, and rural utilities

A Cave Creek desert lot rarely has utilities at the property line, so extending power and building a driveway and access can add real cost and lead time before the house starts. Power runs, a meter spot, and a graded access road are early-schedule items on a rural parcel, alongside the well and septic. See how much it costs to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot , how temporary construction power and the meter spot work , and whether you need a will-serve letter to build in Arizona .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The first move on a Cave Creek custom home is confirming the lot's zoning, water source, and slope before you buy, because Desert Rural setbacks, the well-or-haul water question, and the terrain set both your buildable area and your budget. Confirm whether the parcel is inside the town or in the unincorporated county, since that decides the code. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , how to know if your lot is in the city or unincorporated county , and the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Site prep, builder, and financing

Before a footing pours, a Cave Creek lot usually needs a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, and you call 811 before digging. Pick a licensed builder and line up financing before design locks. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , 811 Blue Stake before digging , how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

Common Cave Creek questions

The most common Cave Creek questions are about codes and accessory dwellings. Start with what building codes apply in Cave Creek for the plain-language code answer, and whether Cave Creek, Fountain Hills, and Carefree allow casitas and ADUs for accessory dwellings. For the whole project arc, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

title: Building a Custom Home in Fountain Hills: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Fountain Hills means designing to the town's 2024 I-codes, installing a required fire-sprinkler system, respecting hillside disturbance limits on sloped lots, and budgeting the town's development impact fee. Expect about $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with hillside estates passing $800. Sprinklers, slope, and the impact fee set it apart from neighboring cities.
bodyHtml:
Building a custom home in Fountain Hills means designing to the town's 2024 family of International Codes, installing a required fire-sprinkler system, respecting hillside disturbance limits on any sloped lot, and budgeting the town's development impact fee. A build typically runs $325 to $650 per square foot in 2026, with hillside estates passing $800. Three local costs set Fountain Hills apart from neighboring cities: the mandatory sprinklers, the hillside rules, and the town impact fee that most neighbors do not charge.

This guide is the Fountain Hills hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Fountain Hills moved to the newest 2024 codes in 2025, so its rules differ from older sets and from neighboring areas still on the 2018 cycle.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Fountain Hills
  • Adopted building codes
  • Required fire sprinklers
  • Permit and impact fees
  • Zoning and setbacks
  • Hillside and disturbance limits
  • Inspections and certificate of occupancy
  • Community design guidelines (FireRock)
  • Buying the lot and starting the build
  • Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage
  • Choosing a builder and financing
  • Common Fountain Hills questions

Cost to build in Fountain Hills

A custom home in Fountain Hills typically runs $325 to $650 per square foot to build in 2026, with hillside estates passing $800. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) Three local costs set it apart: a required fire-sprinkler system, hillside lots with disturbance limits, and a town development impact fee that most neighboring cities do not charge.

These three add up to a meaningful premium over a flat-lot build in a city with no sprinkler mandate and no impact fee. The sprinkler system is a full design-and-install scope, the hillside work means engineered foundations and grading on sloped parcels, and the impact fee is a flat per-home charge set by town resolution. None of these is a finish upgrade you can value-engineer away, so they belong in the budget from the first estimate. The per-foot finish number is the part you control; these three local costs are the part the town and the lot dictate. For the full cost breakdown, including the current impact-fee figure, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Fountain Hills .

Adopted building codes (2024 I-codes)

For permit applications received on or after September 1, 2025, Fountain Hills builds to the 2024 IRC and the rest of the 2024 I-code family, plus the 2023 National Electrical Code, adopted by Ordinance 25-06 with local amendments. The energy path runs on the tighter 2024 IECC as amended. Because the town sits ahead of areas still on the 2018 cycle, a set drawn for an older code is not automatically right for a town lot, and the edition is tied to when the application is received. For the full code list and amendments, see Fountain Hills adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Required fire sprinklers

Fountain Hills requires an automatic fire-sprinkler system in essentially every new home, reaching all levels plus garages and carports. The town adopted this rule in 2008, before Arizona's 2009 ban on new home-sprinkler mandates, so it is grandfathered and still enforced. Most neighboring cities do not require sprinklers, so this is a real cost line a Fountain Hills build carries that others do not, and it affects your mechanical and water budget from the start.

The sprinkler requirement reaches further than people expect. It covers the garage and carport, not just the living space, and the system has to be designed, reviewed, and inspected as its own scope, with a separate review fee. On a lot served by hauled or well water rather than a town main, the water supply and storage have to support the sprinkler demand, which can drive a larger tank or pump. Because the rule predates Arizona's 2009 statute that bars cities from newly mandating home sprinklers, Fountain Hills keeps its mandate while newer cities cannot add one. Budget the system and its review from day one. See Fountain Hills fire-sprinkler requirements and the plain-language answer in do I need fire sprinklers in a new home in Fountain Hills .

Permit and impact fees

Fountain Hills charges a new home three things: a building permit and plan-review fee, a separate fire-sprinkler review fee, and a development impact fee. The impact fee is set by resolution and is the main place a Fountain Hills build costs more than a no-impact-fee neighbor like Paradise Valley. Impact-fee resolutions update and carry effective dates and freeze windows, so confirm the live figure before you budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Fountain Hills building permit and impact fees and, for how permit pricing works across the metro, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot .

Zoning and setbacks

Fountain Hills single-family zoning runs from R1-6 on small lots to R1-190 on one-acre-plus lots. R1-43 needs a 40 foot front yard, 30 foot sides, a 40 foot rear yard, and 20 percent lot coverage. Smaller R1-18 lots need 20, 10, and 30 feet, all under a 30 foot height cap. Your district sets the buildable footprint before any hillside rule shrinks it. For every district's exact yards, coverage, and height, see Fountain Hills residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

Hillside and disturbance limits

Many Fountain Hills lots are sloped, and the town limits how much of a hillside parcel you may disturb, with the steeper ground allowing less grading and clearing. Slope drives engineered foundations, retaining, and cut-and-fill work that a flat lot does not need, which adds cost and shapes where the house can sit. The hillside and disturbance framing affects both your site plan and your budget. The buildable-area limits flow from Fountain Hills residential zoning and setbacks , and the cost effect is detailed in how much it costs to build in Fountain Hills .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Fountain Hills inspects a new home in stages from foundation through final, with a fire-sprinkler inspection added to the usual sequence, and each stage must pass before the work is covered up. After the final inspection passes, the town issues a Certificate of Occupancy. The sprinkler system gets its own rough and final checks, so the inspection list here is one step longer than in a city without a sprinkler mandate. Schedule the sprinkler inspections alongside the framing and final stages so they do not hold up the rest of the build. The permit submittals and the field process are in Fountain Hills residential building permit requirements and the Fountain Hills inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Community design guidelines (FireRock)

Gated Fountain Hills communities like FireRock run their own architectural committee that approves your design before the town does, and its rules are stricter than the town code. FireRock Ridge holds homes to single-story or split-level plans, caps any wall or roofline at 20 feet above natural grade, limits roof slope to 5/12, requires paint at 50 percent LRV or lower, and sets a 2,500 square foot minimum livable area. Plan to these rules from the first sketch. See FireRock Fountain Hills design guidelines , and for the broader luxury-community color rule, LRV paint color rules in north Scottsdale luxury communities .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The first move on a Fountain Hills custom home is confirming the lot's zoning, slope, and any community design rules before you buy, because the district setbacks, hillside disturbance limits, and a community committee like FireRock set your buildable area before design begins. A flat R1-18 lot and a steep hillside R1-43 lot of similar size yield very different homes. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , the steps to building a custom home in Arizona , and what happens during the design phase of a custom home .

Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage

Before a footing pours on a Fountain Hills lot, you usually need a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, with hillside lots adding engineered cut-and-fill work. Call 811 before digging. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , drainage clearance and the Maricopa County grading matrix , and 811 Blue Stake before digging . Plan temporary construction power too.

Choosing a builder and financing

Pick a licensed builder and line up financing before design locks, and make sure your budget carries the sprinkler system and impact fee that a Fountain Hills build adds. Verify the contractor with the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) . See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , what questions to ask a custom home builder before hiring , how to compare custom home builder bids in Arizona , and construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona .

title: Building a Custom Home in Paradise Valley: The Complete Local Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Paradise Valley means designing to the town's older 2015 I-codes, fitting an estate on a one-acre minimum R-43 lot, staying under a 25 percent floor-area cap, and clearing hillside review on sloped ground. bodyHtml:
Building a custom home in Paradise Valley means designing to the town's older 2015 family of International Codes, fitting your estate on a one-acre minimum R-43 lot, staying under a 25 percent floor-area cap, and clearing a Hillside Building Committee review on any sloped ground. A build typically runs $500 to $1,000-plus per square foot in 2026, the highest range in metro Phoenix. The town's one-acre minimum lots, floor-area limit, hillside rules, and well-and-septic reality, not the per-foot finish number, drive the all-in cost.

This guide is the Paradise Valley hub. Each section gives you the short answer, then links the deep page where you can confirm the rules and numbers for your exact lot. Paradise Valley is an estate town with an unusually old code cycle, so a plan drawn for any neighboring city is almost never a clean match.

What this guide covers

  • Cost to build in Paradise Valley

Cost to build in Paradise Valley

A custom home in Paradise Valley typically runs $500 to $1,000-plus per square foot to build in 2026, the highest range in metro Phoenix. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) The driver is not the finish level alone. One-acre minimum lots, the 25 percent floor-area cap, hillside rules, and the well-and-septic reality on lots with no town utilities all add to the all-in number. For the full cost breakdown, see how much it costs to build a custom home in Paradise Valley .

Adopted building codes (2015 I-codes)

Paradise Valley builds to the 2015 IRC and the rest of the 2015 I-code family, plus the 2014 National Electrical Code, with the 2021 International Fire Code layered on top. The town adopted the 2015 codes by Ordinance 2016-02 on May 26, 2016. This is an unusually old cycle, two to three editions behind cities like Phoenix, so a set drawn for a 2021 or 2024 city lot can trigger plan-review corrections here even when the newer work is stronger.

The old cycle has one practical upside and one trap. The upside is lighter paperwork: the 2015 energy code asks for less than a 2024 city's energy path, so your compliance proof is simpler. The trap is reuse. Designers often carry forward a set from a recent Scottsdale or Phoenix job built to newer energy and electrical editions, and those sheets do not match what Paradise Valley adopted, so the plan comes back for corrections. The fire code being a step ahead of the building codes is the wrinkle that catches builders, so confirm fire-access, sprinkler, and water-supply details to the 2021 IFC even while the rest of the set follows 2015. For the exact code list and the local design values, see Paradise Valley adopted building codes and Arizona building code editions by city .

Permit and impact fees

Paradise Valley prices a new-home building permit by construction valuation, set from the building valuation data in the Town's Master Fee Schedule, and in-house plan review adds a percentage of the permit fee on top. The town charges no municipal impact fee, which is one place a Paradise Valley build is lighter than a neighbor like Fountain Hills. Valuation tables and review fees change, so confirm the live numbers before you budget. (volatile:true, confirm closer.) See Paradise Valley building permit and impact fees and, for how valuation pricing works, Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs per square foot .

Zoning, setbacks, and the one-acre minimum

Paradise Valley is an estate town: the base R-43 district requires a full acre per home, with a 40 foot front and rear yard and 20 foot sides. The larger R-175 district needs over four acres and 40 foot yards all around. Most lots cap building height near 24 feet, well below the 30 feet allowed in nearby cities, so the town's homes spread out rather than up. For every district's exact yards, lot size, and height, see Paradise Valley residential zoning and setbacks . For corner parcels, see corner lot and double frontage setbacks in Arizona .

Floor-area ratio and lot coverage

Paradise Valley caps the total floor area of a home at about 25 percent of the lot, a floor-area ratio (FAR) limit that controls how big a house you can build even on a large acre-plus parcel. On a one-acre lot, that cap, the setbacks, and the height limit together set your maximum house size before design begins. This is why two builders on identical lots end up with similar maximum footprints.

The FAR cap is what keeps Paradise Valley feeling like an estate town rather than a wall-to-wall subdivision. A full acre is about 43,560 square feet, so a 25 percent cap points toward a maximum home in the low five figures of square footage before garages, covered patios, and other counted areas eat into the number. Because the cap is a share of the lot, a bigger parcel buys a bigger house, which is part of why land size drives price so directly here. Plan the house to the cap from the first sketch, since a design that overshoots it has to be cut back in review. The setback and coverage figures that feed the FAR math are in Paradise Valley residential zoning and setbacks , and the cost effect of the cap is in how much it costs to build in Paradise Valley .

Hillside development review

Paradise Valley's Hillside Development Regulations (Article XXII) control building on land sloped 10 percent or steeper, and the steeper the lot, the less of it you may disturb. The disturbance allowance drops from 60 percent of the building site at a 10 percent slope to about 34 percent at 15 percent, and into single digits on very steep ground. A Hillside Building Committee reviews every hillside home before the town will permit it. See Paradise Valley hillside development requirements for the slope-to-disturbance table and the committee process.

Well and septic (no town utilities)

A Paradise Valley lot with no town sewer or water routes its wastewater and water approvals through the county and state, not the town. A septic system needs a permit from Maricopa County Environmental Services, starting with a soils evaluation and perc test, and a private well needs authorization from the Arizona Department of Water Resources. These approvals run on their own timelines, so they belong early in the schedule. See Maricopa County septic and well requirements .

Inspections and certificate of occupancy

Paradise Valley inspects a new home in stages from foundation through final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered up. After the final inspection passes, the town issues a Certificate of Occupancy. The permit package and the required plan submittals are detailed in Paradise Valley residential building permit requirements and the Paradise Valley inspections process . The end point is the certificate of occupancy .

Buying the lot and starting the build

The first move on a Paradise Valley custom home is confirming the lot's zoning, slope, and FAR math before you buy, because the one-acre minimum, the 25 percent floor-area cap, and any hillside review set your maximum house before design begins. A flat R-43 acre and a steep hillside acre yield very different homes. Once the land checks out, the project runs through design, permitting, and construction in a known order. See how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , the steps to building a custom home in Arizona , and what happens during the design phase of a custom home .

Site prep: grading, dust, and drainage

Before a footing pours on a Paradise Valley lot, you usually need a grading or earthwork permit, a dust-control permit, and a drainage clearance, with hillside lots adding engineered cut-and-fill work. The desert and hillside site work is its own phase with its own permits. See grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP , dust-control permit , and drainage clearance and the Maricopa County grading matrix . Call 811 Blue Stake before digging , and plan temporary construction power .

Choosing a builder and financing

Pick a licensed builder and line up financing before design locks, because the lender's appraisal and draw schedule shape the timeline, and Paradise Valley's high per-foot range makes the budget unforgiving. Verify the contractor with the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) . See how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , what questions to ask a custom home builder before hiring , and how to compare custom home builder bids in Arizona . On financing, see construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona and construction loan requirements in Arizona .

Common Paradise Valley questions

The most common Paradise Valley questions are about the old code cycle and the estate-lot rules. Start with the steps to building a custom home in Arizona for the full arc, then use the code, zoning, and hillside pages above for the town-specific detail. Because the town's 2015 codes sit so far behind the metro, confirming the edition on your permit is the single most useful first step, covered in Paradise Valley adopted building codes . For climate-smart design on an estate lot, see how to design a custom home to stay cool in the Arizona heat and how long it takes to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

The framing facts in this guide come from the Town of Paradise Valley: the Town Code, Chapter 5 (Building and Construction) , the Building Department , and the Hillside Building Committee . Arizona's broader code-adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This guide is general information, not a code or zoning interpretation. Fees, editions, FAR rules, and hillside limits change, so confirm the current requirements for your parcel with the Paradise Valley Building Department before you rely on them.
