title: Custom Home Warranty, Defects, and Disputes in Arizona: The Complete Guide
summary: A new Arizona custom home is protected by both a written builder warranty and the implied warranty of workmanship and habitability the law gives you automatically. If a defect appears, the Purchaser Dwelling Act sets a notice-and-right-to-repair process before you can sue, the statute of repose caps how long you have, and the ROC complaint and Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund offer routes when a builder fails. This guide maps warranties, defect law, liens, builder insolvency, and the red flags to watch.
bodyHtml:
Custom Home Warranty, Defects, and Disputes in Arizona: The Complete Guide

A new Arizona custom home is protected by two warranties at once: the written builder warranty in your contract, and the implied warranty of workmanship and habitability that Arizona law gives every buyer of a new home even when the contract is silent. When something goes wrong, Arizona's Purchaser Dwelling Act (A.R.S. 12-1361 and following) requires you to give the builder written notice and a chance to repair before you can file suit, and the statute of repose at A.R.S. 12-552 caps the whole window at eight years from substantial completion. If the builder will not stand behind the work, the Registrar of Contractors (ROC) complaint process and the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund are your backstops. This guide maps every one of those rights and links to the deep page behind each.

What this guide covers

  • The written builder warranty
  • Filing a warranty claim and the one-year walkthrough
  • The implied warranty Arizona gives you
  • Construction defects and the Purchaser Dwelling Act
  • The statute of repose: your deadline
  • Attorney fees in a defect claim
  • The ROC complaint and Recovery Fund
  • Mechanics liens on your home
  • When a builder goes broke or walks off
  • Red flags and the first year

The written builder warranty

Your builder's written warranty typically covers workmanship for one year, mechanical systems for two years, and major structural defects for up to ten, though the exact terms come from your specific contract. This express warranty is separate from a third-party "home warranty" service contract you might buy at resale, and the two cover very different things. Read the warranty document before closing so you know what is covered, for how long, and how to make a claim.

Start with new home builder warranty in Arizona and the timeline in how long does a builder warranty last in Arizona? . Know the difference from a service contract in builder warranty vs. home warranty . Some new-home items, like hairline stucco cracks, are normal settling rather than warranty defects, explained in are stucco cracks on a new home normal in Arizona? .

Filing a warranty claim and the one-year walkthrough

Submit a warranty claim in writing through the builder's service process, and schedule a one-year walkthrough before the first-year workmanship coverage ends so nothing slips past the deadline. Most builders run a formal callback system: you log items, the builder inspects, and covered defects get repaired. The one-year mark is the key date because the broadest coverage, workmanship, usually expires then.

Submit correctly with what is a builder warranty service request, and how do I submit a claim? . Understand the callback rhythm in how do builders handle warranty callbacks and the one-year walkthrough? . The glossary defines substantial completion , the date that starts most warranty and legal clocks.

The implied warranty Arizona gives you

Arizona law gives you an implied warranty of workmanship and habitability on a new home automatically, even if your contract never mentions it, and a builder generally cannot make you waive it. This warranty means the home was built in a workmanlike manner and is fit to live in, and Arizona courts have enforced it for decades. It is your legal floor: it exists underneath whatever the written warranty says.

Learn its scope in Arizona implied warranty of workmanship and habitability . Whether a builder can contract around it is settled in can a home builder waive the implied warranty in Arizona? . The glossary defines a construction defect , the trigger for both warranty and defect claims.

Construction defects and the Purchaser Dwelling Act

Before you can sue your builder over a defect, Arizona's Purchaser Dwelling Act requires you to send written notice and give the builder a chance to inspect and repair, a process built into A.R.S. 12-1361 through 12-1363. The act defines a dwelling action and a construction defect, sets the notice content, and gives the builder a fixed window to respond with an offer to repair, pay, or both. Skipping these steps can get a premature lawsuit dismissed, so the notice-and-repair sequence is not optional.

Start with what is the Arizona Purchaser Dwelling Act? and what is a dwelling action in Arizona? . Walk the notice timeline in how Arizona's 90-day notice and right to repair works . The statutes are in the reference library: A.R.S. 12-1361 (definitions) , A.R.S. 12-1362 (notice and repair) , and A.R.S. 12-1363 (builder right to repair) . The glossary defines a dwelling action .

The statute of repose: your deadline

You have at most eight years from substantial completion to bring a construction defect claim in Arizona, under the statute of repose at A.R.S. 12-552, with a narrow extension for defects discovered in the eighth year. A statute of repose is an absolute cutoff that runs from when the work was finished, different from a statute of limitations that runs from when you discover a problem. Once the repose period closes, the claim is gone even if the defect was hidden, so the calendar matters as much as the merits.

Understand the deadline in what is Arizona's statute of repose for construction defects? . The reference library carries A.R.S. 12-552 (statute of repose) , and the glossary defines the statute of repose .

Attorney fees in a defect claim

In an Arizona construction defect or contract dispute, the court can award the winning side its attorney fees, which changes the math on whether to litigate. A.R.S. 12-1364 addresses fees in dwelling actions, and the general contract-dispute fee statute can also apply. Because fees can be recovered either way, both sides have an incentive to settle through the notice-and-repair process rather than fight in court.

See how fees work in can I recover attorney fees in an Arizona construction defect claim? . The reference library carries A.R.S. 12-1364 (construction defect attorney fees) .

The ROC complaint and Recovery Fund

If a licensed builder does poor or unfinished work and will not fix it, you can file a complaint with the Arizona Registrar of Contractors, and if you win and the builder still does not pay, the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund can reimburse part of your loss. The fund pays eligible homeowners up to a per-claim cap, with a larger aggregate cap per license, funded by contractor assessments rather than tax dollars. There is a deadline to file the ROC complaint, so act while the work is recent.

Walk the process in how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim in Arizona , and verify the license first with how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC . The statutes are in the reference library: A.R.S. 32-1132 (recovery fund eligibility) , A.R.S. 32-1132.01 (per-claim cap) , A.R.S. 32-1139 (aggregate cap per license) , and A.R.S. 32-1154 (grounds to discipline a contractor) . The glossary defines the ROC and the contractor bond .

Mechanics liens on your home

A subcontractor or supplier the builder failed to pay can record a mechanics lien against your home, even if you already paid the builder in full, which is one of the biggest risks in any custom build. Arizona's lien law at A.R.S. 33-981 and following gives that right, but A.R.S. 33-1002 provides an important owner-occupant exemption that protects many homeowners who paid their contractor. Collecting lien waivers at each draw is the practical defense.

Start with what is a mechanics lien, and how do lien releases work on a new home in Arizona? . Handle the worst case in how do I remove a mechanics lien from my Arizona home? and the double-payment risk in can a subcontractor put a lien on my house if I already paid the builder in Arizona? . The reference library carries A.R.S. 33-981 (right to a mechanics lien) and A.R.S. 33-1002 (owner-occupied dwelling lien exemption) . The glossary defines the mechanics lien and the lien waiver .

When a builder goes broke or walks off

If your builder goes out of business or abandons the job, your protections are the license bond, any unpaid-work claims, the ROC complaint and Recovery Fund, and the structure of your construction loan draws. The single best defense is built in before trouble: pay only for completed work through the lender's draw process, hold retention, and keep lien waivers current. Once a builder is insolvent, you are competing with other creditors, so prevention beats recovery.

Plan for it with what happens if my builder goes out of business in Arizona? and the related what to do if your builder won't finish your house in Arizona .

Red flags and the first year

Spot the warning signs before you ever sign, because the cheapest defect dispute is the one you avoid by not hiring a bad builder. Unlicensed work, large up-front deposits, no written contract, and pressure to skip the draw process are classic red flags. After move-in, a structured first year of warranty walkthroughs catches the items that the written warranty still covers.

Screen builders with red flags a custom home builder is not legitimate in Arizona . Settle in well with what should I do in the first year in a new custom home? . For choosing a sound builder up front, see our guide to choosing a custom home builder in Arizona .

The takeaway

A new Arizona custom home carries a written builder warranty and an implied warranty the law gives you for free, and a defect claim runs through the Purchaser Dwelling Act's notice-and-repair process inside an eight-year statute of repose. When a builder fails, the ROC complaint, the Recovery Fund, the license bond, and Arizona's lien law are your backstops, and attorney fees can shift to the loser. Each section above links to the deep page and the underlying statute. Keep your warranty document, mind the deadlines, collect lien waivers at every draw, and most disputes never reach a courtroom. The surest protection of all starts earlier, by choosing a licensed, bonded builder in the first place.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 12-1361 (Purchaser Dwelling Act: definitions of dwelling action and construction defect)
  • A.R.S. 12-1363 (Notice, inspection, and the builder's right to repair before a dwelling action)
  • A.R.S. 12-552 (Statute of repose: eight-year limit on construction defect actions)

title: How to Build a Custom Home in Arizona: The Complete Guide
summary: Building a custom home in Arizona moves through eleven connected stages: deciding to build, buying land, setting a budget, getting a construction loan, designing the home, choosing a licensed builder, pulling permits, the build itself, inspections, closing, and the warranty year. A licensed builder runs the whole chain so each handoff happens in the right order, from raw lot to certificate of occupancy.
bodyHtml:
How to Build a Custom Home in Arizona: The Complete Guide

Building a custom home in Arizona runs through eleven connected stages: deciding whether to build, finding and buying land, setting a real budget, securing a construction loan, designing the home, hiring a licensed builder, pulling permits, the build itself, the inspection sequence, closing, and the first warranty year. A licensed builder acts as the hub that links those stages so each one feeds the next in the right order. The work is governed by the 2018 International Residential Code (IRC) as adopted and amended by your city or county, plus Arizona contractor law under the Registrar of Contractors (ROC). This guide walks the whole journey at a high level and links to the deep page for each step so you can go as far down any branch as you need.

What this guide covers

  • Should you build a custom home in Arizona?
  • Finding and buying the land
  • Setting the budget and cost per square foot
  • Financing the build
  • Design, style, and finish selections
  • Choosing and vetting your builder
  • Permits and the pre-construction layer
  • The build: how long it takes and what happens
  • Inspections from footing to final
  • Closing and moving in
  • The warranty year and your rights

Should you build a custom home in Arizona?

Build when you want a specific home on a specific lot and no existing house gets you there; buy when speed and price certainty matter more than control. A custom build trades a longer timeline and more decisions for a home shaped exactly to your site, family, and finishes. The honest first question is money: in many Arizona submarkets, buying an existing home is cheaper per square foot than building new, because builders price today's land, labor, and materials while resale prices reflect older costs.

Start by weighing the trade-offs and confirming the math fits your goals. For the full case on each side, see Is building a custom home worth it in Arizona? and Is it cheaper to buy or build a house in Arizona? . If you are comparing a ready home against a custom path, Move-in ready vs. building custom in Arizona lays out the timeline and control differences. For the shape of the whole project before you commit, read How does the custom home building process work? and What are the steps to building a custom home in Arizona? .

Finding and buying the land

Buy the land before you finalize the design, because the lot decides what you can build and what it will cost to get ready. The single most important fact about any parcel is whether it sits inside an incorporated city or in unincorporated county land, since that sets which building department, codes, and impact fees apply. After that, you confirm utilities (or the lack of them), soil conditions, drainage, easements, and any HOA or design-review rules tied to the lot.

Walk the buying process and the due-diligence checklist in How do I buy land to build a house in Arizona? . To pin down which rules govern your parcel, see How do I know if my lot is in the city or unincorporated county in Arizona? . Arizona's expansive clay soils can drive foundation cost, so most lots need a soils report before design is final. Start with Do I need a soil test to build a house in Arizona? and the deeper Do I need a soils or geotechnical report, and what triggers special foundation requirements in Arizona? . If you are looking at rural acreage, the land brings its own water, septic, and access questions covered in our dedicated guide on building a custom home on rural Arizona land .

Setting the budget and cost per square foot

Set your budget as a total project number with a contingency, not just a price per square foot, because land prep, utilities, design, permits, and finishes all live outside the base build figure. Cost per square foot in Arizona varies widely by location, level of finish, and site difficulty, and a sloped or rural lot can add major site-work cost before the slab is poured. Build a meaningful contingency into the plan so a soils surprise or a finish upgrade does not stall the job, and let the deep cost pages carry the live ranges.

Because these figures move with the market, lean on the deep pages for live ranges. Start with How much does it cost to build a custom home in Arizona? and Cost to build a house per square foot in Arizona . To understand what actually moves the number, read What drives the cost of building a custom home in Arizona? , and if a quote is climbing, Why is my custom home coming in over budget in Arizona? . Story count and city also matter: see Single-story vs. two-story custom home cost in Arizona . The glossary terms allowance and change order explain the two line items that most often move a final price.

Financing the build

Most Arizona custom homes are financed with a construction-to-permanent loan, a single loan that pays for the build in stages and then converts to a normal mortgage when the home is finished. The lender releases money in draws tied to completed work, and an appraiser values the home "as completed" from your plans and specs. You can structure it as one closing (single-close) or two, and the choice affects your rate locks and closing costs.

This whole topic has its own deep guide: construction financing in Arizona . For the core loan, start with Construction-to-permanent loan in Arizona and Construction loan requirements in Arizona . Decide your structure with Single-close vs. two-close construction loan in Arizona , and understand the staged payouts in Construction loan draw schedule explained . For qualifying, see What credit score and down payment do you need for a construction loan in Arizona? . The glossary defines the key terms: draw schedule , interest reserve , and as-completed appraisal .

Design, style, and finish selections

Design turns your ideas into a stamped, permit-ready plan set, and it is where most of the calendar and the personality of the home live. You settle the floor plan and elevations, pick an architectural style suited to the desert, and then make hundreds of finish selections within set budget allowances. In Arizona, smart design also means planning for heat, monsoon drainage, and indoor-outdoor flow from the start, not as add-ons.

For how the phase runs, read What happens during the design phase of a custom home? and How do finish selections work in a custom home? . To choose a look that fits the landscape, see How do I choose an architectural style for my Arizona custom home? and the popular What is desert contemporary architecture, and is it right for Arizona? . For comfort-driven design choices, see How to design a custom home to stay cool in the Arizona heat and How do you design indoor-outdoor living flow for an Arizona custom home? . Whether you need an architect at all is covered in Do I need an architect to build a custom home in Arizona? .

Choosing and vetting your builder

Hire a builder who holds an active Arizona ROC license in the right classification, carries a license bond, and has built homes like yours nearby. The builder is the contractor of record who pulls the permit, schedules the trades, and stands behind the warranty, so this is the most important hire in the project. Verify the license yourself, check references and recent homes in person, and compare bids on scope rather than headline price.

Verify a builder before anything else using How to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and learn the difference in Custom home builder vs. general contractor . Interview well with What questions should I ask a custom home builder before hiring? , and screen for trouble using Red flags a custom home builder is not legitimate in Arizona and What are the green flags of a good custom home builder? . Compare quotes apples-to-apples with How do I compare custom home builder bids and quotes in Arizona? and check the work with How do I check a custom home builder's references and past work? . The glossary explains the contractor's license , the contractor bond , and the ROC itself.

Permits and the pre-construction layer

Before a single footing is dug, your plans go through plan review and you pull a building permit, and on many Arizona lots there is an earlier layer of site permits to clear first. Depending on the parcel, that pre-construction layer can include a dust-control permit, a grading and stormwater plan, a will-serve letter for utilities, temporary construction power, and a floodplain check. Permit timelines and fees vary by city, so confirm both with your local building department.

The site-permit layer is detailed in its own guide: the pre-construction permit layer in Arizona . For the building permit itself, see How long does it take to get a building permit in Arizona? and Building permit cost in Arizona . Know what is required before you dig with Do I need to call 811 Blue Stake before digging on my Arizona property? , and understand the risk of skipping the process in What happens if you build without a permit in Arizona? . The glossary covers plan review and the AHJ , the authority having jurisdiction over your build.

The build: how long it takes and what happens

Construction runs in a fixed sequence: site work and foundation, framing, the mechanical-electrical-plumbing rough-in, insulation and drywall, then interior and exterior finishes. A typical Arizona custom home takes several months to over a year depending on size, site, and selections, with the slab and framing going fast and the finish phase taking the longest. Good communication with your builder, a pre-construction meeting, and milestone walkthroughs keep the job on track.

For realistic timing, see How long does it take to build a custom home in Arizona? . Set the project up right with What is a pre-construction meeting, and what happens there? . Stay involved using How often should I visit my custom home during construction? and How important is communication with your builder during a custom build? . Two key milestone checks are What is a pre-drywall walkthrough on a custom home? and, at the end, What happens at the end of a custom home build? .

Inspections from footing to final

Your city or county inspects the work at set stages, and the job cannot legally advance past a stage until that inspection passes. Typical required inspections include the foundation or setback survey, framing, rough mechanical-electrical-plumbing, insulation, and a final inspection that leads to the certificate of occupancy (CO). A failed inspection means a re-inspection, and some jurisdictions charge a re-inspection fee, so getting it right the first time saves time and money.

See the full sequence in What inspections are required when building a house in Arizona? . Understand failed-inspection costs with What are re-inspection fees, and why am I charged for a failed inspection? . Some elements need a third-party check, explained in What is a special inspection on a custom home, and who pays for it? . If a stop-work order appears, read What is a red tag or stop-work order on an Arizona home build? . The glossary defines the final inspection and the certificate of occupancy .

Closing and moving in

You close on a custom home after the final inspection passes and the certificate of occupancy is issued, which is when a construction-to-permanent loan converts to your regular mortgage. Before move-in, you complete a final walkthrough and a punch list of items the builder must finish or fix, and you receive the certificate that legally lets you occupy the home. The handoff also includes manuals, warranties, and an orientation to the home's systems.

Walk the final stretch with How does closing work on a newly built custom home in Arizona? and When can I move into my new custom home in Arizona? . The closeout steps are covered in What is a punch list and final walkthrough? and What is a builder home orientation or handoff walkthrough? . The glossary explains substantial completion , the punch list , and the temporary certificate of occupancy you may receive if a few items remain.

The warranty year and your rights

A new Arizona custom home comes with both a written builder warranty and Arizona's implied warranty of workmanship and habitability, which the law gives you even if the contract is silent. The written warranty typically covers workmanship for one year, systems for two, and major structural defects for longer, while the implied warranty and the Purchaser Dwelling Act set out how defect claims and the builder's right to repair work. Plan a one-year walkthrough to capture warranty items before the first-year coverage ends.

Start with New home builder warranty in Arizona and How long does a builder warranty last in Arizona? . Know your legal floor with Arizona implied warranty of workmanship and habitability and What is the Arizona Purchaser Dwelling Act? . Handle callbacks with How do builders handle warranty callbacks and the one-year walkthrough? , and settle in using What should I do in the first year in a new custom home? . For the underlying statutes, the reference library carries A.R.S. 12-1361 (Purchaser Dwelling Act definitions) and A.R.S. 12-1363 (builder right to repair) .

The takeaway

A custom home in Arizona is a managed sequence, not a leap: decide, buy land, budget, finance, design, hire a licensed builder, permit, build, inspect, close, and warranty. Each stage above links to a deep page with the real Arizona numbers, codes, and steps, plus three companion guides for financing , the pre-construction permit layer , and rural land . Work the chain in order, keep a contingency, and put a licensed, bonded builder at the center, and the project stays on its rails from raw lot to move-in day.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Planning and Development: Residential Permits and Inspections
  • International Code Council: International Residential Code (model code for one- and two-family dwellings)

title: The Pre-Construction Permit Layer in Arizona: What You Clear Before You Break Ground
summary: Before you pull a building permit in Arizona, most lots have an earlier layer of site permits and clearances to handle: a dust-control permit, a grading and stormwater (SWPPP) plan, a will-serve letter for utilities, temporary construction power, a floodplain-use permit near a wash, and an 811 Blue Stake locate before any digging. Clearing this anticipatory layer in the right order is what lets the build legally start, and skipping it brings red tags and fines.
bodyHtml:
The Pre-Construction Permit Layer in Arizona: What You Clear Before You Break Ground

Before your building permit issues, most Arizona lots carry an earlier layer of site permits and clearances you have to handle in sequence. That anticipatory layer commonly includes a dust-control permit, a grading and stormwater (SWPPP) plan, a will-serve letter confirming utilities, temporary construction power, a floodplain-use permit if you build near a wash, and an 811 Blue Stake locate before any excavation. These are separate from the building permit and are enforced by different agencies, including county air-quality departments, the Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (ADEQ), flood-control districts, and your utility. Clearing them in order is what legally lets the build start; skipping one is how projects get red-tagged and fined. This guide maps the layer and links each step to its deep page.

What this guide covers

  • Why there is a permit layer before the building permit
  • The dust-control permit
  • Grading, SWPPP, and the difference from dust control
  • Stormwater inspections and closing out the SWPPP
  • Will-serve letters for utilities
  • Temporary construction power and the meter spot
  • Floodplain-use permits near a wash
  • 811 Blue Stake before you dig
  • The building permit and plan review
  • What happens if you skip a step

Why there is a permit layer before the building permit

The pre-construction layer exists because preparing a site disturbs soil, air, drainage, and utilities before any house frame goes up, and Arizona regulates each of those separately. Air-quality rules govern the dust your earthmoving kicks up, clean-water rules govern the stormwater that runs off a graded lot, the utility governs whether power and water can even reach the parcel, and flood-control rules govern building near desert washes. Because different agencies issue these, you cannot fold them into one building permit, and the order matters: many lots cannot grade until dust control and stormwater coverage are in place.

For the overall sequence of a build, see the master guide on how to build a custom home in Arizona . The agency that ultimately signs off on your structure, the authority having jurisdiction, is defined in the glossary under AHJ .

The dust-control permit

If your project disturbs enough ground, Arizona requires a dust-control permit before earthmoving begins, because windblown dust is a regulated air pollutant in the Phoenix metro. In Maricopa County, projects that disturb a set threshold of land must obtain the permit and follow control measures such as watering, track-out control, and trained dust personnel. The permit is issued by the county air-quality department, not your city building office, so it is a separate filing.

See whether your project triggers it in Do I need a dust-control permit to build a custom home in Arizona? . The training requirement is explained in the glossary under dust-control training .

Grading, SWPPP, and the difference from dust control

Most custom-home sites also need a grading or earthwork permit and stormwater coverage, often a SWPPP (Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan), before you reshape the lot. The grading permit governs how you cut, fill, and drain the site, while the SWPPP, filed under the state AZPDES construction permit, governs how you keep sediment out of nearby washes and storm drains. People often confuse dust control with the SWPPP, but they are two different programs run by two different agencies: one protects air, the other protects water.

Start with Do I need a grading or earthwork permit and SWPPP before I break ground in Arizona? , then clear up the confusion with Dust-control permit vs. stormwater SWPPP permit in Arizona . For county drainage rules that govern grading, see What is a drainage clearance and the Maricopa County grading matrix? . On sensitive Scottsdale lots, native plants must be salvaged before grading; see What is plant salvage or cactus boxing before grading on a Scottsdale ESL lot? . The glossary defines retention , the on-site stormwater holding many lots require.

Stormwater inspections and closing out the SWPPP

Once a SWPPP is active, it is not a one-time filing; you must inspect and maintain the stormwater controls on a set schedule throughout the build and then formally close the permit at the end. Inspections typically happen on a regular interval and after rain events, and lapses can draw enforcement under the clean-water program. When the home is done and the soil is stabilized, you file a notice of termination to end coverage.

See the inspection cadence in How often must stormwater SWPPP controls be inspected during an Arizona build? , and the closeout in What is the AZPDES notice of termination and final stabilization at the end of a build? .

Will-serve letters for utilities

Before a building department will approve plans on many lots, you need a will-serve letter, a written commitment from the utility or water provider that it will supply service to your parcel. The letter proves the home will have water, sewer, or power and is often a condition of permit approval, especially on the edge of a service area or on rural land. Without it, plan review can stall because the city cannot confirm the home will be served.

Learn what it is and when you need one in What is a will-serve letter, and do I need one to build in Arizona? . On rural parcels, utilities may not exist at all, which is covered in the rural-land guide on building a custom home on rural Arizona land and in How much does it cost to extend power or utilities to a rural Arizona lot? .

Temporary construction power and the meter spot

The crew needs electricity before the home is wired, so you arrange temporary construction power through a temporary pole and a utility-set meter, a step that has its own inspection and a coordinated "meter spot" with the power company. The power company sets the temporary meter only after the local building department approves the temporary service, so the two have to be sequenced. This is easy to overlook and can delay framing if it is not ordered early.

See how it works and how to time it in How does temporary construction power and the meter spot work on a new Arizona home? .

Floodplain-use permits near a wash

If your lot is in or near a mapped floodplain or a desert wash, you likely need a floodplain-use permit before building, on top of the standard building permit. The flood-control district reviews how the home and grading affect flood flows and may require the finished floor to sit above a base flood elevation. Building near a wash without this clearance is a common and costly oversight in Maricopa County.

See whether your parcel needs one in Do I need a floodplain-use permit to build near a wash in Maricopa County? . Flood-zone status also affects financing and insurance, covered in Do I need flood insurance on a construction loan in Arizona? .

811 Blue Stake before you dig

Arizona law requires you to call 811 (Blue Stake) before any digging so utilities can mark their buried lines, and this applies to your excavation, trenching, and grading. The locate is free, has a required waiting period before you dig, and protects you from striking a gas, power, or water line, an act that carries liability and danger. Skipping it is both illegal and the kind of mistake that injures crews and triggers fines.

See exactly when and how to call in Do I need to call 811 Blue Stake before digging on my Arizona property? . The glossary defines Blue Stake .

The building permit and plan review

After the site layer is handled, your house plans go through plan review and the city or county issues the building permit that authorizes the actual structure. Review checks the plans against the adopted building code, and timelines and fees vary widely by jurisdiction. Some elements, such as fire sprinklers or trusses, come in later as deferred submittals, which can add time if not planned for.

See timing in How long does it take to get a building permit in Arizona? and cost in Building permit cost in Arizona . Understand late-arriving plan packages in What is a deferred submittal, and how can it delay my build? , and the first inspection trigger in What is a setback or foundation-location spot survey, and why does the inspector require one? . To track an existing permit, see How to look up a building permit in Arizona . The reference library carries the jurisdiction detail in Maricopa County residential building permit requirements and the fee basis in Arizona building permit fees: valuation vs. per square foot . The glossary defines plan review .

What happens if you skip a step

Skipping any part of this layer can stop your project cold, because each clearance gates work that follows it. Grading without a dust permit or SWPPP, digging without an 811 locate, or building near a wash without a floodplain permit can all draw a red tag or stop-work order, fines, and required corrective work. The cheapest path is always to clear the layer in order before the equipment shows up.

See the consequences in What happens if you build without a permit in Arizona? and the enforcement tool in What is a red tag or stop-work order on an Arizona home build? . For the inspection sequence that follows, see What inspections are required when building a house in Arizona? .

The takeaway

The pre-construction permit layer is the quiet first half of any Arizona build: dust control, grading and SWPPP, a will-serve letter, temporary power, floodplain clearance, and an 811 locate, all cleared before the building permit puts a frame in the air. Each section links to a deep page with the live thresholds, fees, and agency rules. Handle them in order and the build starts clean; ignore one and you invite red tags and delay. For the whole project around this layer, see the master guide on how to build a custom home in Arizona .

Sources

  • Maricopa County Air Quality: Fugitive Dust from Dust-Generating Operations (Rule 310 dust permits)
  • Arizona Department of Environmental Quality: AZPDES Construction Activity General Permit (CGP) for Stormwater
  • Arizona Corporation Commission: Underground Facilities Law (Arizona 811 / Blue Stake call before you dig)
